name: Macanese Creole (Patuá)

ancestors.notes: European Portuguese primary lexifier; Macanese community spoke a Portuguese-based koine from 16th century Macau.
Malay adstrate: Macau's early population included Malay-speaking servants and traders; Malay contributed vocabulary and some phonological features (final nasal stops, word-initial ng-).
Cantonese adstrate: Cantonese-speaking population of Macau. Cantonese contributed final stops (-p/-t/-k) and tonal awareness, though Patuá has no phonemic tone.

notes: Macanese Creole (Patuá / Maquista). Portuguese-based creole of the Macanese (Eurasian) community of Macau. Contact history: Portuguese from 1557, with Malay, Cantonese, Konkani, and Sinhalese speakers. Nearly extinct — fewer than 50 fluent speakers as of 2020, most elderly. KEY FEATURES: (1) FINAL STOPS: Cantonese-influenced final -p/-t/-k are preserved in loanwords, unlike European PT. (2) WORD-INITIAL NG-: Malay-derived words allow initial /ŋ/ (ngapi, etc.). (3) SIMPLIFIED CONSONANT CLUSTERS: PT clusters reduced (e.g., tr→t, pr→p in many words). (4) NO TONES: despite Cantonese influence, Patuá is not tonal. (5) PALATAL CONSONANTS: ch=[tʃ], lh=[ʎ], nh=[ɲ], j=[ʒ], x=[ʃ] from PT. (6) DIPHTHONGS: ai=[aj], au=[aw] preserved. Language is critically endangered; revitalization efforts led by the Dóci Papiaçám di Macau theatre group.

sources.author: Baxter, A.N.
sources.title: Portuguese and Creole Portuguese in the Pacific and Western Pacific Rim
sources.publisher: In: Atlas of Languages of Intercultural Communication, Mouton

sources.author: Pinharanda-Nunes, M.
sources.title: Patuá — Língua Crioula de Macau
sources.notes: Contemporary description of phonology and lexicon
